title: Print inorder traversal (recursive)
description: Print the inorder traversal (Left -> Root -> Right) of a binary tree using recursion

examples.input: Tree: 1 (root), left: 2, right: 3
examples.explanation: Step 1: Start at root 1. Not null. Step 2: Recursively call inorder on left child 2. Step 3: At node 2: Not null. Step 4: Recursively call inorder on left child of 2 (null). Step 5: Return from null left child. Step 6: Print 2. Step 7: Recursively call inorder on right child of 2 (null). Step 8: Return from null right child. Step 9: Return to node 1. Step 10: Print 1. Step 11: Recursively call inorder on right child 3. Step 12: At node 3: Not null. Step 13: Recursively call inorder on left child of 3 (null). Step 14: Return from null left child. Step 15: Print 3. Step 16: Recursively call inorder on right child of 3 (null). Step 17: Return from null right child. Step 18: Return to node 1. Step 19: Return from root. Final output: 2 1 3.

examples.input: Tree: 4 (root), left: 2 (left: 1, right: 3), right: 6 (left: 5, right: 7)
examples.explanation: Step 1: Start at root 4. Step 2: Recursively call inorder on left subtree rooted at 2. Step 3: At node 2: Recursively call inorder on left child 1. Step 4: At node 1: Left child null, return. Print 1. Right child null, return. Step 5: Return to node 2: Print 2. Step 6: Recursively call inorder on right child 3. Step 7: At node 3: Left null, return. Print 3. Right null, return. Step 8: Return to node 2, then to node 4. Step 9: Print 4. Step 10: Recursively call inorder on right subtree rooted at 6. Step 11: At node 6: Recursively call inorder on left child 5. Step 12: At node 5: Left null, return. Print 5. Right null, return. Step 13: Return to node 6: Print 6. Step 14: Recursively call inorder on right child 7. Step 15: At node 7: Left null, return. Print 7. Right null, return. Step 16: Return to node 6, then to node 4. Final output: 1 2 3 4 5 6 7.

approaches.name: Approach 1: Recursive Inorder Traversal
approaches.summary: This approach uses recursion to traverse the binary tree in inorder order: recursively process the left subtree, then the root node, then the right subtree. It is straightforward and leverages the natural recursive structure of trees.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(h) where h is the height of the tree (due to recursion stack)

ladders.title: Full Problem — Recursive Inorder Traversal of Binary Tree
ladders.desc: Complete solution to print inorder traversal of a binary tree using recursion
ladders.explanation: This is the complete problem where we implement a recursive inorder traversal to print node values in Left → Root → Right order. The intuition is that binary trees have a natural recursive structure: each node has left and right subtrees that are themselves binary trees. By recursively processing the left subtree first, then the current node, then the right subtree, we achieve the inorder ordering. The algorithm starts at the root: if the node is null, return (base case). Otherwise: 1) Recursively call inorder on left child, 2) Print current node's value, 3) Recursively call inorder on right child. Time complexity is O(n) where n is number of nodes, as each node is visited exactly once. Space complexity is O(h) where h is tree height, due to recursion stack depth. This connects to L1 as it's the complete implementation using the recursive helper. Common mistakes include forgetting base case (causing infinite recursion), printing before left recursion (preorder), or after right recursion (postorder). Real code pattern: def inorder(root): if not root: return; inorder(root.left); print(root.val); inorder(root.right).

examples.input: Tree: 1 (root), left: 2, right: 3
examples.trace: Step 1: Start at root 1. Not null. Step 2: Recursively call inorder on left child 2. Step 3: At node 2: Not null. Step 4: Recursively call inorder on left child of 2 (null). Step 5: Return from null left child. Step 6: Print 2. Step 7: Recursively call inorder on right child of 2 (null). Step 8: Return from null right child. Step 9: Return to node 1. Step 10: Print 1. Step 11: Recursively call inorder on right child 3. Step 12: At node 3: Not null. Step 13: Recursively call inorder on left child of 3 (null). Step 14: Return from null left child. Step 15: Print 3. Step 16: Recursively call inorder on right child of 3 (null). Step 17: Return from null right child. Step 18: Return to node 1. Step 19: Return from root. Final output: 2 1 3.

examples.input: Tree: 4 (root), left: 2 (left: 1, right: 3), right: 6 (left: 5, right: 7)
examples.trace: Step 1: Start at root 4. Step 2: Recursively call inorder on left subtree rooted at 2. Step 3: At node 2: Recursively call inorder on left child 1. Step 4: At node 1: Left child null, return. Print 1. Right child null, return. Step 5: Return to node 2: Print 2. Step 6: Recursively call inorder on right child 3. Step 7: At node 3: Left null, return. Print 3. Right null, return. Step 8: Return to node 2, then to node 4. Step 9: Print 4. Step 10: Recursively call inorder on right subtree rooted at 6. Step 11: At node 6: Recursively call inorder on left child 5. Step 12: At node 5: Left null, return. Print 5. Right null, return. Step 13: Return to node 6: Print 6. Step 14: Recursively call inorder on right child 7. Step 15: At node 7: Left null, return. Print 7. Right null, return. Step 16: Return to node 6, then to node 4. Final output: 1 2 3 4 5 6 7.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Inorder Traversal Function
ladders.desc: Implement the recursive function that performs inorder traversal on a binary tree node
ladders.explanation: This is the core recursive function that implements the inorder traversal logic. The intuition is that each node in a binary tree can be processed by handling its left subtree, then itself, then its right subtree. The function takes a tree node as input: if the node is null, it returns immediately (base case). Otherwise, it recursively calls itself on the left child, processes the current node (prints its value), then recursively calls itself on the right child. This creates a depth-first traversal that follows Left → Root → Right order. Time complexity is O(n) where n is number of nodes in the subtree rooted at the input node. Space complexity is O(h) where h is height of that subtree, due to recursion stack. This connects to L0 as it's the main function called from the complete solution. Common mistakes include incorrect base case handling or wrong order of recursive calls. Real code pattern: def inorder(node): if node is None: return; inorder(node.left); print(node.value); inorder(node.right).

examples.input: Node: 5 with left child 3 and right child 7
examples.trace: Step 1: Call inorder(5). Node not null. Step 2: Recursively call inorder(3). Step 3: At inorder(3): Node not null. Step 4: Recursively call inorder(left child of 3 - null). Step 5: Return from null. Step 6: Print 3. Step 7: Recursively call inorder(right child of 3 - null). Step 8: Return from null. Step 9: Return to inorder(5). Step 10: Print 5. Step 11: Recursively call inorder(7). Step 12: At inorder(7): Node not null. Step 13: Recursively call inorder(left child of 7 - null). Step 14: Return from null. Step 15: Print 7. Step 16: Recursively call inorder(right child of 7 - null). Step 17: Return from null. Step 18: Return to inorder(5). Final output: 3 5 7.

examples.input: Node: 10 with left child 5 (left: 3, right: 7) and right child 15
examples.trace: Step 1: Call inorder(10). Step 2: Recursively call inorder(5). Step 3: At inorder(5): Recursively call inorder(3). Step 4: At inorder(3): Left null, return. Print 3. Right null, return. Step 5: Return to inorder(5): Print 5. Step 6: Recursively call inorder(7). Step 7: At inorder(7): Left null, return. Print 7. Right null, return. Step 8: Return to inorder(5), then to inorder(10). Step 9: Print 10. Step 10: Recursively call inorder(15). Step 11: At inorder(15): Left null, return. Print 15. Right null, return. Step 12: Return to inorder(10). Final output: 3 5 7 10 15.

testCases.input: Node: 4 with left child 2 and right child 6
Node: 10 with left child 5 (left: 3, right: 7) and right child 15
Node: 20 with left child 10 (left: 5, right: 15) and right child 30 (left: 25, right: 35)

ladders.title: Core Logic — Inorder Traversal Order: Left, Root, Right
ladders.desc: Understand and implement the specific sequence of operations for inorder traversal
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the core logic of inorder traversal: the specific order in which nodes are processed. The intuition is that for any binary tree node, we must process all nodes in its left subtree first, then the node itself, then all nodes in its right subtree. This ordering preserves the 'inorder' property where for binary search trees, it yields values in sorted order. The algorithm for a node is: 1) Process left subtree completely, 2) Process current node, 3) Process right subtree completely. This is implemented recursively by making recursive calls in that order. Time complexity for processing a subtree is O(k) where k is number of nodes in that subtree. Space complexity is O(h) for recursion depth. This connects to L1 as it defines the order within the recursive function. Common mistakes include swapping the order (e.g., root before left gives preorder) or missing steps. Real code pattern: process_left_subtree(); process_current_node(); process_right_subtree().

examples.input: Node with value 10, left subtree values [5, 3, 7], right subtree values [15, 12, 18]
examples.trace: Step 1: For node 10, first process left subtree rooted at 5. Step 2: For node 5, process its left subtree: node 3. Step 3: Node 3 has no left/right children, so process 3. Step 4: Back to node 5: after left subtree done, process node 5. Step 5: Process right subtree of 5: node 7. Step 6: Node 7 has no children, process 7. Step 7: Left subtree of 10 complete: output so far: 3 5 7. Step 8: Process node 10. Step 9: Process right subtree of 10 rooted at 15. Step 10: For node 15, process left subtree: node 12. Step 11: Node 12 has no children, process 12. Step 12: Back to node 15: process node 15. Step 13: Process right subtree of 15: node 18. Step 14: Node 18 has no children, process 18. Step 15: Final output: 3 5 7 10 12 15 18.

examples.input: Node with value 1, left subtree null, right subtree values [2, 3]
examples.trace: Step 1: For node 1, process left subtree: null, so nothing. Step 2: Process node 1. Step 3: Process right subtree rooted at 2. Step 4: For node 2, process left subtree: null. Step 5: Process node 2. Step 6: Process right subtree of 2: node 3. Step 7: For node 3, process left subtree: null. Step 8: Process node 3. Step 9: Process right subtree of 3: null. Step 10: Final output: 1 2 3.

ladders.title: Building Block — Recursive Tree Traversal Pattern
ladders.desc: Implement a generic recursive function that traverses a binary tree
ladders.explanation: This level introduces the fundamental pattern of recursively traversing a binary tree. The intuition is that trees are recursive data structures: each node has left and right children that are themselves trees (or null). Therefore, tree operations naturally lend themselves to recursion. The algorithm is: for a given node, if it's null, return (base case). Otherwise, perform some operation on the node, and recursively apply the same function to its left and right children. The specific order of these operations determines the traversal type (inorder, preorder, postorder). Time complexity is O(n) for visiting all nodes. Space complexity is O(h) for recursion stack. This connects to L2 as it provides the recursive framework into which we plug the inorder order. Common mistakes include missing base case or infinite recursion. Real code pattern: def traverse(node): if node is None: return; # Do something with node; traverse(node.left); traverse(node.right).

examples.input: Tree: 1 (root), left: 2, right: 3. Operation: print node value.
examples.trace: Step 1: Call traverse(1). Node not null. Step 2: Print 1. Step 3: Recursively call traverse(2). Step 4: At traverse(2): Node not null. Step 5: Print 2. Step 6: Recursively call traverse(left of 2 - null). Step 7: Return from null. Step 8: Recursively call traverse(right of 2 - null). Step 9: Return from null. Step 10: Return to traverse(1). Step 11: Recursively call traverse(3). Step 12: At traverse(3): Node not null. Step 13: Print 3. Step 14: Recursively call traverse(left of 3 - null). Step 15: Return from null. Step 16: Recursively call traverse(right of 3 - null). Step 17: Return from null. Step 18: Return to traverse(1). Final output: 1 2 3 (this is preorder as we print before recursing).

examples.input: Tree: 4 (root), left: 2, right: 6. Operation: print node value after children.
examples.trace: Step 1: Call traverse(4). Node not null. Step 2: Recursively call traverse(2). Step 3: At traverse(2): Node not null. Step 4: Recursively call traverse(left of 2 - null). Step 5: Return from null. Step 6: Recursively call traverse(right of 2 - null). Step 7: Return from null. Step 8: Print 2. Step 9: Return to traverse(4). Step 10: Recursively call traverse(6). Step 11: At traverse(6): Node not null. Step 12: Recursively call traverse(left of 6 - null). Step 13: Return from null. Step 14: Recursively call traverse(right of 6 - null). Step 15: Return from null. Step 16: Print 6. Step 17: Return to traverse(4). Step 18: Print 4. Final output: 2 6 4 (postorder).

testCases.input: Tree: 5 with left child 3, operation: print
Tree: 5 with right child 7, operation: print
Tree: 5 with left child 3 and right child 7, operation: print
Tree: 10 with left child 5 (left: 3, right: 7) and right child 15, operation: print

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Accessing Binary Tree Node Children
ladders.desc: Retrieve left and right child pointers from a binary tree node
ladders.explanation: This level covers the fundamental operation of accessing a binary tree node's children. In a binary tree, each node typically has references to its left and right children (which may be null). The intuition is that to traverse a tree, we need to move from a node to its children. The algorithm is straightforward: given a node object, use node.left to get the left child and node.right to get the right child. These operations are O(1) time and O(1) space. This connects to L3 as it's how we navigate the tree in recursive traversal. Common mistakes include not checking for null before accessing children (leading to errors) or confusing left/right. Real code pattern: left_child = node.left; right_child = node.right.

examples.input: Node with value 10, left child pointer to node with value 5, right child pointer to node with value 15
examples.output: Left child value: 5, Right child value: 15
examples.trace: Step 1: Given node object 'node' with value 10. Step 2: Access node.left, which returns a reference to the left child node. Step 3: That left child node has value 5. Step 4: Access node.right, which returns a reference to the right child node. Step 5: That right child node has value 15. Step 6: Output shows left child value is 5, right child value is 15.

examples.input: Node with value 7, left child null, right child pointer to node with value 9
examples.output: Left child: null, Right child value: 9
examples.trace: Step 1: Given node with value 7. Step 2: Access node.left, which returns null (indicating no left child). Step 3: Access node.right, which returns a reference to the right child node. Step 4: That right child node has value 9. Step 5: Output shows left child is null, right child value is 9.

testCases.expected: Error: Cannot access children of null

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Binary Tree Node Structure
ladders.desc: Understand what a binary tree node is and how to represent it
ladders.explanation: This is the foundational concept: a binary tree node is a basic data structure that represents one element in a binary tree. Each node typically contains: 1) A value (e.g., integer, string), 2) A reference/pointer to its left child (another node or null), 3) A reference/pointer to its right child (another node or null). The intuition is that nodes are connected to form a tree structure, with one node designated as the root. A null reference indicates no child (leaf node or missing child). This is O(1) space per node. This connects to L4 as it defines the object whose children we access. Common mistakes include not initializing children to null or misunderstanding references. Real code pattern: class Node: def __init__(self, val): self.val = val; self.left = None; self.right = None.

examples.input: Create a node with value 10
examples.output: Node object with val=10, left=None, right=None
examples.trace: Step 1: Define a Node class with attributes val, left, right. Step 2: Create an instance: node = Node(10). Step 3: The constructor sets node.val = 10. Step 4: It sets node.left = None (default). Step 5: It sets node.right = None (default). Step 6: Result is a node object storing value 10 with no children.

examples.input: Create a node with value 5, left child node with value 3, right child node with value 7
examples.output: Node object with val=5, left points to node(3), right points to node(7)
examples.trace: Step 1: Create left child node: left_node = Node(3). Step 2: Create right child node: right_node = Node(7). Step 3: Create parent node: parent_node = Node(5). Step 4: Set parent_node.left = left_node. Step 5: Set parent_node.right = right_node. Step 6: Result: parent_node.val = 5, parent_node.left points to node with val=3, parent_node.right points to node with val=7.

approaches.name: Approach 2: Iterative Stack-Based Inorder Traversal
approaches.summary: This approach uses an explicit stack to simulate the recursion, iteratively traversing the tree by pushing nodes onto the stack and processing them in inorder order. It avoids recursion overhead and can be useful in environments with limited stack space.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(h) where h is the height of the tree (due to stack storage)

ladders.title: Full Problem — Iterative Stack-Based Inorder Traversal
ladders.desc: Complete iterative solution using explicit stack to print inorder traversal of binary tree
ladders.explanation: This approach simulates the recursive inorder traversal process using an explicit stack data structure. The intuition is that recursion implicitly uses the call stack to track nodes to process later - we can explicitly manage this stack ourselves. The algorithm maintains two pointers: current node pointer and a stack. We start with current pointing to root. While current is not null OR stack is not empty: 1) If current is not null, push current onto stack and move current to current.left (go deep left). 2) When current becomes null, pop from stack (this is the next node in inorder), process it (print), then set current to popped_node.right. This ensures we process nodes in Left-Root-Right order. Time complexity is O(n) as we visit each node exactly once. Space complexity is O(h) where h is tree height, as stack stores at most one path from root to leaf. This avoids recursion overhead and stack overflow risks. Connection to L1: This integrates the stack management logic with tree traversal. Common mistakes include forgetting to check both current AND stack in loop condition, or incorrectly updating current after popping. Real pattern: while current or stack: if current: stack.append(current); current = current.left else: node = stack.pop(); print(node.val); current = node.right

examples.input: Tree: 1 (root), left: 2, right: 3; 2 has left: 4, right: 5
examples.trace:
Step 1: current=1, stack=[], push 1, current=1.left=2
Step 2: current=2, stack=[1], push 2, current=2.left=4
Step 3: current=4, stack=[1,2], push 4, current=4.left=None
Step 4: current=None, stack=[1,2,4], pop 4, print 4, current=4.right=None
Step 5: current=None, stack=[1,2], pop 2, print 2, current=2.right=5
Step 6: current=5, stack=[1], push 5, current=5.left=None
Step 7: current=None, stack=[1,5], pop 5, print 5, current=5.right=None
Step 8: current=None, stack=[1], pop 1, print 1, current=1.right=3
Step 9: current=3, stack=[], push 3, current=3.left=None
Step 10: current=None, stack=[3], pop 3, print 3, current=3.right=None
Step 11: current=None, stack=[], loop ends

examples.input: Tree: 10 (root), left: 5, right: 20; 5 has left: 3, right: 7; 20 has left: 15
examples.trace:
Step 1: current=10, stack=[], push 10, current=10.left=5
Step 2: current=5, stack=[10], push 5, current=5.left=3
Step 3: current=3, stack=[10,5], push 3, current=3.left=None
Step 4: current=None, stack=[10,5,3], pop 3, print 3, current=3.right=None
Step 5: current=None, stack=[10,5], pop 5, print 5, current=5.right=7

Step 7: current=None, stack=[10,7], pop 7, print 7, current=7.right=None

Step 9: current=20, stack=[], push 20, current=20.left=15
Step 10: current=15, stack=[20], push 15, current=15.left=None
Step 11: current=None, stack=[20,15], pop 15, print 15, current=15.right=None
Step 12: current=None, stack=[20], pop 20, print 20, current=20.right=None
Step 13: current=None, stack=[], loop ends

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Stack Management for Tree Traversal
ladders.desc: Implement stack operations to track nodes during tree traversal
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine focuses on the stack management logic that enables iterative tree traversal. The intuition is that we need to remember nodes to process later when we go deep into left subtrees. The stack serves as memory for nodes whose right subtrees haven't been explored yet. The algorithm: Initialize empty stack and current pointer. While current is not null OR stack is not empty: If current exists, push it onto stack and go left. When current becomes null, pop from stack (this gives us the next node in inorder), process it, then explore its right subtree. Time complexity depends on number of push/pop operations (O(n)). Space is O(h) for stack storage. Connection to L0: This is the core control flow of the full solution. Common mistakes: Not checking both conditions in loop, or popping when stack is empty. Real pattern: stack = []; current = root; while current or stack: if current: stack.append(current); current = current.left else: node = stack.pop(); process(node); current = node.right

examples.input: Tree: 1->2->3 (left chain), stack operations
examples.output: Push 1, Push 2, Push 3, Pop 3, Pop 2, Pop 1
examples.trace:
Step 1: current=1, stack=[], push 1, current=1.left=2
Step 2: current=2, stack=[1], push 2, current=2.left=3
Step 3: current=3, stack=[1,2], push 3, current=3.left=None
Step 4: current=None, stack=[1,2,3], pop 3, process 3, current=3.right=None
Step 5: current=None, stack=[1,2], pop 2, process 2, current=2.right=None
Step 6: current=None, stack=[1], pop 1, process 1, current=1.right=None
Step 7: current=None, stack=[], loop ends

examples.input: Tree: 10 with left 5 and right 20, stack operations
examples.output: Push 10, Push 5, Pop 5, Pop 10, Push 20, Pop 20
examples.trace:
Step 1: current=10, stack=[], push 10, current=10.left=5
Step 2: current=5, stack=[10], push 5, current=5.left=None
Step 3: current=None, stack=[10,5], pop 5, process 5, current=5.right=None
Step 4: current=None, stack=[10], pop 10, process 10, current=10.right=20
Step 5: current=20, stack=[], push 20, current=20.left=None
Step 6: current=None, stack=[20], pop 20, process 20, current=20.right=None
Step 7: current=None, stack=[], loop ends

ladders.title: Core Logic — Inorder Traversal Order Simulation
ladders.desc: Understanding and implementing Left-Root-Right processing order iteratively
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the core insight: how to achieve Left-Root-Right order without recursion. The intuition is that inorder traversal means: completely process left subtree first, then current node, then right subtree. Iteratively, we achieve this by: 1) Going as deep left as possible (pushing nodes to stack). 2) When we can't go left further, the last pushed node is the leftmost - we process it. 3) Then we explore its right subtree (which repeats the process). This creates the correct order because we always finish left subtree before processing root. The algorithm: Use current pointer to track position. While we can go left, push nodes and go left. When stuck, pop (process leftmost), then set current to its right child. Time O(n), space O(h). Connection to L1: This explains WHY the stack management produces inorder order. Common mistakes: Processing node before going left completely, or not handling right subtree after popping. Real pattern: Go left until null, then pop and process, then go right.

examples.input: Tree: A (root), left: B, right: C; process order
examples.trace:
Step 1: Start at A, go left to B (push A)
Step 2: At B, go left (null)
Step 3: Pop B (process B) - left subtree done
Step 4: Go to B.right (null)
Step 5: Pop A (process A) - root processed
Step 6: Go to A.right = C
Step 7: At C, go left (null)
Step 8: Pop C (process C) - right subtree done

examples.input: Tree: 5, left: 3 (has left: 1), right: 8; process order
examples.trace:
Step 1: Start at 5, go left to 3 (push 5)
Step 2: At 3, go left to 1 (push 3)
Step 3: At 1, go left (null)
Step 4: Pop 1 (process 1) - leftmost leaf
Step 5: Go to 1.right (null)
Step 6: Pop 3 (process 3) - left subtree of 5 done
Step 7: Go to 3.right (null)
Step 8: Pop 5 (process 5) - root
Step 9: Go to 5.right = 8
Step 10: At 8, go left (null)
Step 11: Pop 8 (process 8) - right subtree

Tree: 100 with left 50 (has left 25) and right 150 (has left 125), process order
Tree: 8 with left 4 (has left 2, right 6) and right 12 (has left 10), process order

ladders.title: Building Block — Tree Node Navigation with Pointers
ladders.desc: Navigating binary tree structure using left/right pointers
ladders.explanation: This building block teaches how to move through a binary tree using node pointers. The intuition is that each tree node has left and right child pointers (possibly null). We need to understand how to: start at root, move to left child, move to right child, and detect when we've reached a leaf (both children null). The algorithm: Represent node as object with val, left, right attributes. Use current pointer variable. To go left: current = current.left. To go right: current = current.right. Check if current is null to know we've reached end of path. Time O(1) per navigation, space O(1). Connection to L2: This enables the 'go left until null' and 'go right' steps in inorder logic. Common mistakes: Not checking for null before accessing left/right, or infinite loops when structure has cycles. Real pattern: class Node: def __init__(self, val): self.val = val; self.left = None; self.right = None. Navigation: if current: current = current.left

examples.input: Tree: Node(1) with left Node(2), right Node(3). Navigate from root to left to right
examples.output: Start at 1, go left to 2, go back to 1, go right to 3
examples.trace:
Step 1: current = root (node with val 1)
Step 2: current = current.left (node with val 2)
Step 3: current = node with val 2
Step 4: Reset current to root
Step 5: current = current.right (node with val 3)
Step 6: current = node with val 3

examples.input: Tree: Node(5) with left Node(3) which has left Node(1). Navigate left twice
examples.output: Start at 5, go left to 3, go left to 1, attempt third left gives null
examples.trace:
Step 1: current = root (val 5)
Step 2: current = current.left (val 3)
Step 3: current = current.left (val 1)
Step 4: current = current.left (None, because node 1 has no left child)
Step 5: current is null

testCases.input: Tree: Node(10), navigate left from root
Tree: Node(7) with left Node(4), navigate left from root
Tree: Node(9) with right Node(12), navigate right from root
Tree: Node(6) with left Node(2) and right Node(8), navigate left then right from root
testCases.expected: From root to Node(2), back to root, to Node(8)

testCases.input: Tree: Node(15) with left Node(10) which has left Node(5), navigate left twice
testCases.expected: From root to Node(10) to Node(5)

testCases.input: Tree: Node(20) with right Node(30) which has right Node(40), navigate right twice
testCases.expected: From root to Node(30) to Node(40)

testCases.input: Tree: Node(50) with left Node(30) and right Node(70), navigate left then attempt right from left node
testCases.expected: From root to Node(30), Node(30).right is null

testCases.input: Tree: Node(100) with left Node(50) which has right Node(75), navigate left then right
testCases.expected: From root to Node(50) to Node(75)

testCases.expected: From root to Node(3) to Node(6)

testCases.input: Tree: Node(25) with right Node(35) which has left Node(30), navigate right then left
testCases.expected: From root to Node(35) to Node(30)

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Stack Push and Pop Operations
ladders.desc: Implementing stack data structure operations: push (add to top) and pop (remove from top)
ladders.explanation: This level covers the fundamental stack operations needed for the traversal. The intuition is that stack is a LIFO (Last-In-First-Out) structure - perfect for remembering nodes to process later. Push adds element to top, pop removes and returns top element. The algorithm: Use list to represent stack. Push: stack.append(item). Pop: if stack not empty, return stack.pop(). Check empty: len(stack) == 0. Time O(1) for push/pop, space O(n) for storage. Connection to L3: Stack stores nodes during navigation. Common mistakes: Popping from empty stack, or using wrong end of list for stack operations. Real pattern: stack = []; stack.append(x); y = stack.pop() if stack else None

examples.input: Push 5, Push 3, Push 7, then Pop three times
examples.trace:
Step 1: stack = []
Step 2: push 5 -> stack = [5]
Step 3: push 3 -> stack = [5,3]
Step 4: push 7 -> stack = [5,3,7]
Step 5: pop -> returns 7, stack = [5,3]
Step 6: pop -> returns 3, stack = [5]
Step 7: pop -> returns 5, stack = []

examples.input: Push A, Pop, Push B, Push C, Pop, Pop
examples.output: Operations: push A, pop returns A, push B, push C, pop returns C, pop returns B
examples.trace:
Step 1: stack = []
Step 2: push A -> stack = [A]
Step 3: pop -> returns A, stack = []
Step 4: push B -> stack = [B]
Step 5: push C -> stack = [B,C]
Step 6: pop -> returns C, stack = [B]
Step 7: pop -> returns B, stack = []

testCases.input: Push X, Push Y, pop, pop
Push 1, Push 2, Push 3, pop, pop, pop
Push A, pop, push B, pop
Push 5, Push 10, pop, Push 15, pop, pop
Push M, Push N, Push P, pop, push Q, pop, pop
Push 100, Push 200, pop, push 300, push 400, pop, pop
Push red, push blue, push green, pop, pop, push yellow, pop, pop

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Binary Tree Structure
ladders.desc: Understanding what a binary tree is: nodes with at most two children
ladders.explanation: This foundational level explains the binary tree data structure. The intuition is that a binary tree is a hierarchical structure where each node has at most two children: left and right. It starts with a root node. Nodes without children are leaves. The tree can be empty (null). Key concepts: Root (top node), Parent/Child relationships, Leaf (node with no children), Subtree (tree formed by a node and its descendants). Visualize as upside-down tree. The structure: class Node with attributes: value, left_child, right_child. Left and right can be null. Time/space not applicable here as it's structure definition. Connection to L4: Stack will store these node objects. Common mistakes: Confusing binary tree with binary search tree (BST has ordering), or thinking trees must be balanced. Real pattern: class TreeNode: def __init__(self, val): self.val = val; self.left = None; self.right = None

examples.input: Simple binary tree: root A with left child B and right child C
examples.trace:
Step 1: Create node A with value 'A'
Step 2: Create node B with value 'B'
Step 3: Create node C with value 'C'
Step 4: Set A.left = B
Step 5: Set A.right = C
Step 6: B.left = None, B.right = None
Step 7: C.left = None, C.right = None
Result: Tree with 3 nodes, root A has two children

examples.input: Binary tree: root 5 with left child 3 (which has left child 1), and right child 8
examples.trace:
Step 1: Create node 5
Step 2: Create node 3
Step 3: Create node 8
Step 4: Create node 1
Step 5: Set 5.left = 3
Step 6: Set 5.right = 8
Step 7: Set 3.left = 1
Step 8: Set 3.right = None
Step 9: Set 1.left = None, 1.right = None
Step 10: Set 8.left = None, 8.right = None
Result: Tree with 4 nodes, height 2

Binary tree: root 7 with left child 4
Binary tree: root 9 with right child 12

Binary tree: root 15 with left child 10 which has left child 5
Binary tree: root 20 with right child 30 which has right child 40

approaches.name: Approach 3: Morris Inorder Traversal
approaches.summary: This approach uses threading to traverse the tree without recursion or an explicit stack, by temporarily modifying the tree structure to create links to predecessors. It achieves inorder traversal with constant extra space.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Morris Inorder Traversal Implementation
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of Morris Inorder Traversal to print values without recursion or explicit stack
ladders.explanation: Morris Inorder Traversal is an elegant algorithm that achieves O(n) time complexity with O(1) extra space by temporarily modifying the tree structure. The core insight is to create temporary links (threads) from the rightmost node of a node's left subtree back to the node itself, allowing us to traverse back up without a stack. The algorithm works by: 1) Starting at the root, 2) If the current node has no left child, we process it and move to its right child, 3) If it has a left child, we find its inorder predecessor (the rightmost node in the left subtree), 4) If the predecessor's right pointer is null, we set it to point to the current node (creating a thread) and move to the left child, 5) If the predecessor's right pointer already points to the current node (thread exists), we break the thread, process the current node, and move to the right child. This approach essentially simulates the recursive call stack through these temporary threads. The time complexity is O(n) because each node is visited at most twice (once to create threads, once to process). Space complexity is O(1) as we only use a few pointers. Common mistakes include: forgetting to break threads after use (causing infinite loops), not handling null trees properly, and incorrect predecessor finding. This algorithm is particularly useful in memory-constrained environments where recursion depth or stack size is limited.

examples.input: Tree: [1,2,3,4,5,null,6] (level order)
examples.trace:
Step 1: Start at root 1. Left child exists (2). Find predecessor: left subtree root=2, rightmost=5. 5.right=null, set 5.right=1 (thread). Move to 2.
Step 2: Current=2. Left child exists (4). Find predecessor: left subtree root=4, rightmost=4. 4.right=null, set 4.right=2 (thread). Move to 4.
Step 3: Current=4. No left child. Print 4. Move to right (thread to 2).
Step 4: Current=2. Left child exists but predecessor 4.right=2 (thread exists). Break thread: 4.right=null. Print 2. Move to right (5).
Step 5: Current=5. No left child. Print 5. Move to right (thread to 1).
Step 6: Current=1. Left child exists but predecessor 5.right=1 (thread exists). Break thread: 5.right=null. Print 1. Move to right (3).
Step 7: Current=3. No left child. Print 3. Move to right (6).
Step 8: Current=6. No left child. Print 6. Move to right (null). Done.

examples.input: Tree: [5,3,8,1,4,7,9] (level order)
examples.trace:
Step 1: Start at root 5. Left child exists (3). Find predecessor: left subtree root=3, rightmost=4. 4.right=null, set 4.right=5 (thread). Move to 3.
Step 2: Current=3. Left child exists (1). Find predecessor: left subtree root=1, rightmost=1. 1.right=null, set 1.right=3 (thread). Move to 1.
Step 3: Current=1. No left child. Print 1. Move to right (thread to 3).
Step 4: Current=3. Left child exists but predecessor 1.right=3 (thread exists). Break thread: 1.right=null. Print 3. Move to right (4).
Step 5: Current=4. No left child. Print 4. Move to right (thread to 5).
Step 6: Current=5. Left child exists but predecessor 4.right=5 (thread exists). Break thread: 4.right=null. Print 5. Move to right (8).
Step 7: Current=8. Left child exists (7). Find predecessor: left subtree root=7, rightmost=7. 7.right=null, set 7.right=8 (thread). Move to 7.
Step 8: Current=7. No left child. Print 7. Move to right (thread to 8).
Step 9: Current=8. Left child exists but predecessor 7.right=8 (thread exists). Break thread: 7.right=null. Print 8. Move to right (9).
Step 10: Current=9. No left child. Print 9. Move to right (null). Done.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Finding and Managing Inorder Predecessor Threads
ladders.desc: Identifying the inorder predecessor and creating/breaking temporary threads
ladders.explanation: The key sub-routine in Morris Traversal is finding the inorder predecessor of a node and managing the temporary threads to and from it. For a given node, its inorder predecessor is the rightmost node in its left subtree. This sub-routine has two main operations: 1) When we need to create a thread: we find the predecessor, check if its right pointer is null, and if so, set it to point to the current node. This creates a temporary link that allows us to return to the current node after processing the left subtree. 2) When we encounter an existing thread: we break it by setting the predecessor's right pointer back to null, process the current node, and move to its right child. The intuition is that these threads simulate the return from recursive calls in a standard inorder traversal. Time complexity for this sub-routine is O(h) in the worst case where h is the height of the left subtree, but amortized over the entire traversal it becomes O(1) per node. Space complexity is O(1). Common mistakes include: not checking if the predecessor's right pointer already points to the current node (leading to cycles), incorrectly identifying the predecessor (must be rightmost in left subtree), and forgetting to break threads after use. This sub-routine directly enables the O(1) space characteristic of Morris Traversal by replacing the explicit stack with these temporary links.

examples.input: Node: 5 with left subtree [3,1,4], find predecessor and create thread
examples.trace:
Step 1: Start at node 5. Left child is 3.
Step 2: Move to left child 3.
Step 3: While current.right exists and current.right ≠ 5: current = current.right.
Step 4: At node 3: 3.right = 4, move to 4.
Step 5: At node 4: 4.right = null, stop. Predecessor = 4.
Step 6: Check 4.right = null, so create thread: 4.right = 5.

examples.input: Node: 3 with existing thread from predecessor 1 (1.right = 3), break thread
examples.trace:
Step 1: Start at node 3. Left child is 1.
Step 2: Find predecessor: start at left child 1.
Step 3: While current.right exists and current.right ≠ 3: current = current.right.
Step 4: At node 1: 1.right = 3, so current.right = 3, stop. Predecessor = 1.
Step 5: Check 1.right = 3 (not null), so thread exists.
Step 6: Break thread: set 1.right = null.
Step 7: Process node 3 (print value).

testCases.input: Node: 1, left: null, existing thread from elsewhere

ladders.title: Core Logic — Thread-Based Tree Traversal Without Stack
ladders.desc: Using temporary threads to simulate recursive traversal with constant space
ladders.explanation: The core logic of Morris Traversal is the thread-based mechanism that eliminates the need for an explicit stack or recursion. The algorithm maintains a current pointer and uses the following logic: 1) If the current node has no left child, we process it and move to its right child (either actual right child or a thread). 2) If it has a left child, we find its inorder predecessor. 3) If the predecessor's right pointer is null, we set it to the current node (creating a thread) and move current to the left child. 4) If the predecessor's right pointer is already the current node (thread exists), we break the thread, process the current node, and move current to its right child. The intuition is that threads create temporary cycles in the tree that allow us to return to higher nodes after processing their left subtrees, exactly mimicking how a call stack works in recursive traversal. Each node is visited at most twice: once to create a thread (when we go down to its left subtree) and once to process it (when we return via the thread). The space complexity is O(1) because we only use a few pointers (current, predecessor) rather than storing an entire call stack. Time complexity is O(n) as each edge is traversed a constant number of times. This approach is particularly elegant because it modifies the tree temporarily but restores it completely by the end. Common mistakes include infinite loops from not breaking threads, and incorrect traversal order from misidentifying when to process nodes.

examples.input: Tree: [2,1,3], demonstrate thread creation and usage
examples.trace:
Step 1: Current=2, has left child 1. Find predecessor: start at 1, 1.right=null, so predecessor=1. 1.right=null, create thread: 1.right=2. Move current to 1.
Step 2: Current=1, no left child. Process 1. Move to right (thread to 2).
Step 3: Current=2, has left child but predecessor 1.right=2 (thread exists). Break thread: 1.right=null. Process 2. Move to right (3).
Step 4: Current=3, no left child. Process 3. Move to right (null). Done.

examples.input: Tree: [4,2,5,1,3], show multiple thread operations
examples.trace:
Step 1: Current=4, left child 2. Find predecessor: start at 2, go right to 3, 3.right=null, predecessor=3. Create thread: 3.right=4. Move to 2.
Step 2: Current=2, left child 1. Find predecessor: start at 1, 1.right=null, predecessor=1. Create thread: 1.right=2. Move to 1.
Step 3: Current=1, no left child. Process 1. Move to right (thread to 2).
Step 4: Current=2, left child exists but predecessor 1.right=2 (thread exists). Break thread: 1.right=null. Process 2. Move to right (3).
Step 5: Current=3, no left child. Process 3. Move to right (thread to 4).
Step 6: Current=4, left child exists but predecessor 3.right=4 (thread exists). Break thread: 3.right=null. Process 4. Move to right (5).
Step 7: Current=5, no left child. Process 5. Move to right (null). Done.

testCases.expected: Process 1, move right to 2, process 2, move right to 3, process 3

Create thread 1.right=2, process 1, break thread, process 2, create thread 2.right=3, process 2? Wait, trace needed: Actually: Current=3, left=2, find pred=1, thread 1.right=3, move to 2; current=2, left=1, find pred=1, thread exists? 1.right=3 not 2, so create thread 1.right=2, move to 1; current=1, process 1, move to thread 2; current=2, break thread 1.right=null, process 2, move to thread 3; current=3, break thread 1.right=null? No, 1.right already null, actually predecessor for 3 is 1? Let's simplify: Expected traversal 1 2 3
Create threads as needed, process inorder

testCases.input: Tree where predecessor has right child
testCases.expected: Predecessor found correctly despite right child

ladders.title: Building Block — Inorder Predecessor in Binary Tree
ladders.desc: Finding the node that comes immediately before a given node in inorder traversal
ladders.explanation: The inorder predecessor of a node in a binary tree is the node that would be visited immediately before it in an inorder traversal. For a node with a left child, the predecessor is the rightmost node in that left subtree. For a node without a left child, the predecessor is the first ancestor for which the node is in the right subtree (but in Morris traversal, we primarily deal with nodes that have left children). Finding the predecessor involves starting at the left child and repeatedly moving to right children until we reach a node with no right child or until we circle back to the original node (in case a thread exists). The intuition is that in inorder traversal, after fully traversing a left subtree, we visit the root, so the last node visited in the left subtree is the immediate predecessor. Time complexity is O(h) where h is the height of the left subtree, but in Morris traversal this cost is amortized. Space complexity is O(1). This concept is crucial for Morris traversal because we use the predecessor to create threads: we temporarily modify its right pointer to point to the current node, creating a path back. Common mistakes include: confusing predecessor with successor, not handling null left child cases, and infinite loops when threads exist. Understanding predecessor finding is essential for implementing the thread mechanism correctly.

examples.input: Node: 5 with left subtree root 3, subtree nodes: 3,1,4
examples.trace:
Step 1: Start at left child of 5, which is 3.
Step 2: Initialize pred = 3.
Step 3: While pred.right exists and pred.right ≠ 5: move pred to pred.right.
Step 4: pred = 3, 3.right = 4, and 4 ≠ 5, so pred = 4.
Step 5: pred = 4, 4.right = null, so stop. Predecessor = 4.

examples.input: Node: 2 with left subtree root 1, existing thread 1.right = 2
examples.trace:
Step 1: Start at left child of 2, which is 1.
Step 2: Initialize pred = 1.
Step 3: While pred.right exists and pred.right ≠ 2: check condition.
Step 4: pred = 1, 1.right = 2, so pred.right = 2, condition pred.right ≠ 2 is false, stop.
Step 5: Predecessor = 1, and we note that 1.right = 2 (thread exists).

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Tree Pointer Manipulation
ladders.desc: Fundamental operations of following and modifying pointers in a binary tree
ladders.explanation: Tree pointer manipulation involves basic operations on binary tree nodes: following pointers (left, right) and modifying pointers (setting left or right to new values). In a binary tree, each node typically has three fields: value, left child pointer, and right child pointer. Following pointers means accessing node.left or node.right to move through the tree. Modifying pointers means changing where these pointers point, such as setting node.right = otherNode. In Morris traversal, we use these operations extensively: we follow left pointers to go down the tree, follow right pointers to go to right children or threads, and modify right pointers to create and break threads. The intuition is that by temporarily changing the right pointer of certain nodes (their inorder predecessors), we create a path back to ancestors, simulating a stack. It's crucial to restore pointers after use to maintain the original tree structure. Time complexity for each pointer operation is O(1). Space complexity is O(1) per operation. Common mistakes include: losing references by overwriting pointers without saving them, creating cycles by incorrect pointer assignments, and not handling null pointers properly. These operations are the building blocks for any tree algorithm and must be mastered before attempting advanced techniques like Morris traversal.

examples.input: Node A with left=B, right=C. Follow left, then modify right.
examples.output: Follow: A.left gives B. Modify: set B.right = D.
examples.trace:
Step 1: Start with node A.
Step 2: Follow left pointer: node = A.left, which is B.
Step 3: Now at node B. Its current right pointer might be null or point to something.
Step 4: Modify right pointer: B.right = D (where D is another node).
Step 5: Result: B now points to D as its right child.

examples.input: Node X with right=Y. Follow right, then check if null.
examples.output: Follow: X.right gives Y. Check: Y is not null.
examples.trace:
Step 1: Start with node X.
Step 2: Follow right pointer: next = X.right, which is Y.
Step 3: Check if next is null: Y ≠ null, so we can proceed.
Step 4: If we modify: we could set Y.left = Z, but in this example just following.
Step 5: Result: We've moved from X to Y via the right pointer.

testCases.input: Node: val=1, left=null, right=null. Follow left.
Node: val=2, left=Node(1), right=Node(3). Follow right.
Node: val=4, left=Node(2), right=Node(6). Modify left to Node(3).
Node: val=5, left=null, right=Node(7). Modify right to null.
Node: val=3, left=Node(1), right=Node(4). Follow left then right of that node.
Node: val=8, left=Node(5), right=null. Check if right is null.
Node: val=6, left=null, right=Node(9). Modify right to Node(10).
Node: val=7, left=Node(4), right=Node(8). Follow left, modify its right.
Node: val=9, left=Node(6), right=Node(12). Follow right, check its left.
Node: val=10, left=null, right=null. Modify left to Node(8).

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Binary Tree Node Structure
ladders.desc: Understanding the basic building block of a binary tree: nodes with value and pointers
ladders.explanation: A binary tree node is the fundamental unit of a binary tree, consisting of three components: 1) A value (or data) that stores the node's information, 2) A left pointer that references the left child node (or is null if no left child exists), and 3) A right pointer that references the right child node (or is null if no right child exists). In many programming languages, this is implemented as a class or struct with these fields. The intuition is that each node can have at most two children, arranged in a hierarchical structure. The left pointer typically points to a node with a smaller value (in binary search trees) or simply the left subtree, and the right pointer to a node with a larger value or the right subtree. Understanding this structure is essential because all tree operations—traversal, insertion, deletion—are built upon accessing and manipulating these pointers. Time complexity for accessing a node's value or pointers is O(1). Space complexity per node is O(1) for the pointers plus the value. Common mistakes include: confusing left and right pointers, not initializing pointers to null, and misunderstanding that pointers are references (not the nodes themselves). This foundation is critical for Morris traversal because the algorithm relies on temporarily modifying the right pointers to create threads, and understanding that these are just references that can be changed and restored.

examples.input: Create a node with value 5, no children
examples.trace:
Step 1: Define a node structure with fields: val, left, right.
Step 2: Set val = 5.
Step 3: Initialize left = null (no left child).
Step 4: Initialize right = null (no right child).
Step 5: Result is a leaf node with value 5.

examples.input: Node with value 3, left child value 1, right child value 4
examples.trace:
Step 1: Create root node with val=3.
Step 2: Create left child node with val=1, left=null, right=null.
Step 3: Set root.left = reference to left child node.
Step 4: Create right child node with val=4, left=null, right=null.
Step 5: Set root.right = reference to right child node.
Step 6: Result is a node with two children.

testCases.input: Node with value 7 and left child 5
Node with value 2 and right child 3
Node with value 9, left child 8, right child 10
Node with value 6, no left child, right child 9
Node with value 4, left child 2, no right child
Node with value 12, left child 11, right child 13
Node with value 15, left child 14, right child 16
